question_repaired: What is A1C, and how does it work?
reference_answer: Summary : A1C is a blood test for type 2 diabetes and prediabetes. It measures your average blood glucose, or blood sugar, level over the past 3 months. Doctors may use the A1C alone or in combination with other diabetes tests to make a diagnosis. They also use the A1C to see how well you are managing your diabetes. This test is different from the blood sugar checks that people with diabetes do every day. Your A1C test result is given in percentages. The higher the percentage, the higher your blood sugar levels have been: - A normal A1C level is below 5.7 percent - Prediabetes is between 5.7 to 6.4 percent. Having prediabetes is a risk factor for getting type 2 diabetes. People with prediabetes may need retests every year. - Type 2 diabetes is above 6.5 percent - If you have diabetes, you should have the A1C test at least twice a year. The A1C goal for many people with diabetes is below 7. It may be different for you. Ask what your goal should be. If your A1C result is too high, you may need to change your diabetes care plan. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

It's commonly used to diagnose diabetes or prediabetes and monitor long-term glucose control in individuals with diabetes. An HbA1c level below 5.7% indicates normal blood sugar regulation; between 5.7% - 6.4% suggests prediabetes, while an HbA1c above 6.5% typically signifies diabetes when confirmed through repeated testing. The American Diabetes Association recommends routine monitoring for people with type 2 diabetes at least twice per year if well controlled, quarterly otherwise. Regular physical activity can help lower HbA1c values along with medication adherence as prescribed by healthcare providers.

question_noisy: what is that painful swollen thing with pus?
question_repaired: What could cause a painful swollen lump that contains pus?
reference_answer: An abscess is a pocket of pus. You can get an abscess almost anywhere in your body. When an area of your body becomes infected, your body's immune system tries to fight the infection. White blood cells go to the infected area, collect within the damaged tissue, and cause inflammation. During this process, pus forms. Pus is a mixture of living and dead white blood cells, germs, and dead tissue. Bacteria, viruses, parasites and swallowed objects can all lead to abscesses. Skin abscesses are easy to detect. They are red, raised and painful. Abscesses inside your body may not be obvious and can damage organs, including the brain, lungs and others. Treatments include drainage and antibiotics.

It's important for individuals with these symptoms to seek prompt evaluation and treatment from healthcare professionals.

question_repaired: What exactly is an acoustic neuroma?
reference_answer: An acoustic neuroma is a benign tumor that develops on the nerve that connects the ear to the brain. The tumor usually grows slowly. As it grows, it presses against the hearing and balance nerves. At first, you may have no symptoms or mild symptoms. They can include - Loss of hearing on one side - Ringing in ears - Dizziness and balance problems The tumor can also eventually cause numbness or paralysis of the face. If it grows large enough, it can press against the brain, becoming life-threatening. Acoustic neuroma can be difficult to diagnose, because the symptoms are similar to those of middle ear problems. Ear exams, hearing tests, and scans can show if you have it. If the tumor stays small, you may only need to have it checked regularly. If you do need treatment, surgery and radiation are options. If the tumors affect both hearing nerves, it is often because of a genetic disorder called neurofibromatosis. NIH: National Institute on Deafness and Communication Disorders
answer_clean: An acoustic neuroma, also known as vestibular schwannoma, is a noncancerous tumor that develops on the eighth cranial nerve leading from your inner ear to your brain. The branches of this nerve directly influence hearing and balance functions; hence these types of benign growth can cause issues such as hearing loss or ringing in one's ears, imbalance, dizziness among others if they grow large enough. Treatment options include monitoring for smaller ones, radiation therapy, or surgical removal depending upon its size, location, patient’s age, overall health etc.
answer_noisy: Acoustic neuromas (also known as vestibular schwannomas) are noncancerous tumors that develop from the Schwann cells covering the eighth cranial nerve near its connection to the inner ear. question_repaired: What is the medical term for a short-term irritation or infection of the breathing tubes that causes a bad cough and lots of mucus but is not pneumonia?
reference_answer: Bronchitis is an inflammation of the bronchial tubes, the airways that carry air to your lungs. It causes a cough that often brings up mucus. It can also cause shortness of breath, wheezing, a low fever, and chest tightness. There are two main types of bronchitis: acute and chronic. Most cases of acute bronchitis get better within several days. But your cough can last for several weeks after the infection is gone. The same viruses that cause colds and the flu often cause acute bronchitis. These viruses spread through the air when people cough, or though physical contact (for example, on unwashed hands). Being exposed to tobacco smoke, air pollution, dusts, vapors, and fumes can also cause acute bronchitis. Less often, bacteria can also cause acute bronchitis. To diagnose acute bronchitis, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and listen to your breathing. You may also have other tests. Treatments include rest, fluids, and aspirin (for adults) or acetaminophen to treat fever. A humidifier or steam can also help. You may need inhaled medicine to open your airways if you are wheezing. Antibiotics won't help if the cause is viral. You may get antibiotics if the cause is bacterial. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

Treatment typically involves rest, hydration, over-the-counter pain relievers, and sometimes antibiotics if it’s determined to have been caused by bacterial infection.

question_noisy: What is acute leukemia, exactly?
question_repaired: What exactly is acute leukemia?
reference_answer: Leukemia is cancer of the white blood cells. White blood cells help your body fight infection. Your blood cells form in your bone marrow. In leukemia, however, the bone marrow produces abnormal white blood cells. These cells crowd out the healthy blood cells, making it hard for blood to do its work. In acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), also called acute lymphoblastic leukemia, there are too many of specific types of white blood cells called lymphocytes or lymphoblasts. ALL is the most common type of cancer in children. Possible risk factors for ALL include being male, being white, previous chemotherapy treatment, exposure to radiation, and for adults, being older than 70. Symptoms of ALL include: - Weakness or feeling tired - Fever - Easy bruising or bleeding - Bleeding under the skin - Shortness of breath - Weight loss or loss of appetite - Pain in the bones or stomach - Pain or a feeling of fullness below the ribs - Painless lumps in the neck, underarm, stomach, or groin Tests that examine the blood and bone marrow diagnose ALL. Treatments include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, stem cell transplants, and targeted therapy. Targeted therapy uses substances that attack cancer cells without harming normal cells. Once the leukemia is in remission, you need additional treatment to make sure that it does not come back. NIH: National Cancer Institute
answer_clean: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), also known as acute lymphoid or lymphoblastic leukemia, is an aggressive type of cancer that affects white blood cells called B-cells and T-cells in their early stages of development within bone marrow. It progresses rapidly if left untreated but can often be treated successfully with chemotherapy and other therapies such as stem cell transplantation. ALL primarily occurs in children under 5 years old; however, it may occur at any age. Symptoms include fatigue, fever, bleeding/bruising easily, frequent infections, painless swelling of limbs due to enlarged livers or spleens, weight loss without trying, recurrent nosebleeds, rashes, joint pains, etc. The exact cause remains unknown though genetic predisposition plays some role along with exposure to radiation or certain chemical agents like benzene. Diagnosis involves physical examination followed by laboratory tests including complete blood count(CBC), peripheral smear review, flow cytometry analysis for immunophenotyping, molecular testing using polymerase chain reaction (PCR). Treatment options depend upon factors like patient's age, overall health status & subtype classification which includes standard risk vs high risk groups determined through various progn

It requires immediate treatment because it can rapidly become life threatening if left untreated. There are several types classified by which kind of cell they resemble under microscope examination - most common being AML (acute myeloid) or ALL (acute lymphoblastic). Treatment typically involves chemotherapy with possible stem cell transplantation for eligible patients depending upon disease subtype, patient's age, overall health status etc.

question_noisy: What does it mean when stuff inside your body kind of sticks together after something, like is that the same as those internal scar-band things?
question_repaired: What does it mean when tissues or organs inside the body stick together after surgery or inflammation, and is that the same as internal scar tissue bands called adhesions?
reference_answer: Adhesions are bands of scar-like tissue. Normally, internal tissues and organs have slippery surfaces so they can shift easily as the body moves. Adhesions cause tissues and organs to stick together. They might connect the loops of the intestines to each other, to nearby organs, or to the wall of the abdomen. They can pull sections of the intestines out of place. This may block food from passing through the intestine. Adhesions can occur anywhere in the body. But they often form after surgery on the abdomen. Almost everyone who has surgery on the abdomen gets adhesions. Some adhesions don't cause any problems. But when they partly or completely block the intestines, they cause symptoms such as - Severe abdominal pain or cramping - Vomiting - Bloating - An inability to pass gas - Constipation Adhesions can sometimes cause infertility in women by preventing fertilized eggs from reaching the uterus. No tests are available to detect adhesions. Doctors usually find them during surgery to diagnose other problems. Some adhesions go away by themselves. If they partly block your intestines, a diet low in fiber can allow food to move easily through the affected area. If you have a complete intestinal obstruction, it is life threatening. You should get immediate medical attention and may need surgery. NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

reference_answer: Amblyopia, or "lazy eye," is the most common cause of visual impairment in children. It happens when an eye fails to work properly with the brain. The eye may look normal, but the brain favors the other eye. In some cases, it can affect both eyes. Causes include - Strabismus - a disorder in which the two eyes don't line up in the same direction - Refractive error in an eye - when one eye cannot focus as well as the other, because of a problem with its shape. This includes nearsightedness, farsightedness, and astigmatism. - Cataract - a clouding in the lens of the eye It can be hard to diagnose amblyopia. It is often found during a routine vision exam. Treatment for amblyopia forces the child to use the eye with weaker vision. There are two common ways to do this. One is to have the child wear a patch over the good eye for several hours each day, over a number of weeks to months. The other is with eye drops that temporarily blur vision. Each day, the child gets a drop of a drug called atropine in the stronger eye. It is also sometimes necessary to treat the underlying cause. This could include glasses or surgery. NIH: National Eye Institute
answer_clean: Amblyopia, also known as "lazy eye," refers to reduced vision in one or both eyes that cannot be corrected with glasses alone. If untreated during childhood, this condition can lead to permanent loss of depth perception and binocular vision later in life. question_noisy: what is that chest/heart thing people mean when they say angina, like is it just pain or some kind of attack or blood flow problem or what?

reference_answer: Angina is chest pain or discomfort you feel when there is not enough blood flow to your heart muscle. Your heart muscle needs the oxygen that the blood carries. Angina is a symptom of coronary artery disease (CAD), the most common heart disease. CAD happens when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply blood to the heart, reducing blood flow. There are three types of angina: - Stable angina is the most common type. It happens when the heart is working harder than usual. Stable angina has a regular pattern. Rest and medicines usually help. - Unstable angina is the most dangerous. It does not follow a pattern and can happen without physical exertion. It does not go away with rest or medicine. It is a sign that you could have a heart attack soon. - Variant angina is rare. It happens when you are resting. Medicines can help. Not all chest pain or discomfort is angina. If you have chest pain, you should see your health care provider. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

There are two main types - stable angina which follows predictable patterns with exertion; and unstable angina which may signal an impending myocardial infarction (heart attack). Treatment includes lifestyle changes, medications like nitrates for symptom relief, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers among others, and possibly revascularization procedures like percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), commonly known as stenting, or coronary artery bypass graft surgery depending upon severity & extent of CAD. reference_answer: Summary : If you have coronary artery disease, the arteries in your heart are narrowed or blocked by a sticky material called plaque. When the tube is in place, the doctor inflates the balloon to push the plaque outward against the wall of the artery. This widens the artery and restores blood flow. Doctors may use angioplasty to - Reduce chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart - Minimize damage to heart muscle from a heart attack Many people go home the day after angioplasty, and are able to return to work within a week of coming home. Often stents are placed during this process as well. It helps alleviate symptoms of heart disease like chest pain and can prevent more serious conditions such as myocardial infarction. Once positioned correctly using X-ray guidance, this tiny balloon tip expands within the blockage which compresses fatty deposits against the walls of the artery allowing improved blood flow. Often afterward, a metal mesh called a "stent" may be placed permanently inside the newly widened section of the artery to keep it from closing again. However, patients must continue taking medications prescribed postoperatively including antiplatelet drugs to prevent clot formation around the new device. answer_repaired:
Angioplasty works by inserting a catheter with an inflatable balloon at its tip into the narrowed part of the blood vessel through small incisions near the groin area. Once positioned correctly using X-ray imaging for guidance, the balloon is then expanded which compresses the plaque against the walls of the arteries thereby widening them allowing better blood flow. In some cases, a stent (a tiny wire mesh tube) may be placed during this process as well to help keep the artery open long term after deflation of the balloon.

reference_answer: Summary : Antibiotics are medicines that fight bacterial infections. Used properly, they can save lives. But there is a growing problem of antibiotic resistance. It happens when bacteria change and become able to resist the effects of an antibiotic. Using antibiotics can lead to resistance. Each time you take antibiotics, sensitive bacteria are killed. But resistant germs may be left to grow and multiply. They can spread to other people. They can also cause infections that certain antibiotics cannot cure. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is one example. It causes infections that are resistant to several common antibiotics. To help prevent antibiotic resistance - Don't use antibiotics for viruses like colds or flu. Antibiotics don't work on viruses. - Don't pressure your doctor to give you an antibiotic. - When you take antibiotics, follow the directions carefully. Finish your medicine even if you feel better. If you stop treatment too soon, some bacteria may survive and re-infect you. - Don't save antibiotics for later or use someone else's prescription. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

question_noisy: what is that aneurysm thing in the big artery, like what does it mean?
question_repaired: What is an aneurysm in a major artery, and what does it mean?
reference_answer: An aneurysm is a bulge or "ballooning" in the wall of an artery. Arteries are blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood from the heart to other parts of the body. If an aneurysm grows large, it can burst and cause dangerous bleeding or even death. Most aneurysms are in the aorta, the main artery that runs from the heart through the chest and abdomen. There are two types of aortic aneurysm: - Thoracic aortic aneurysms - these occur in the part of the aorta running through the chest - Abdominal aortic aneurysms - these occur in the part of the aorta running through the abdomen Most aneurysms are found during tests done for other reasons. Some people are at high risk for aneurysms. It is important for them to get screening, because aneurysms can develop and become large before causing any symptoms Screening is recommended for people between the ages of 65 and 75 if they have a family history, or if they are men who have smoked. Doctors use imaging tests to find aneurysms. Medicines and surgery are the two main treatments. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
answer_clean: An aortic aneurysm is a bulge or dilation in the wall of the major blood vessel that carries oxygenated blood from your heart to other parts of your body (the aorta). It can occur anywhere along the length of the artery but most commonly occurs below the kidneys where it becomes wider naturally due to branching vessels called bifurcations. If left untreated, there's risk for rupture which could lead to life-threatening internal bleeding. The exact cause isn’t always known, though factors like high blood pressure, smoking, genetic predisposition may contribute. Treatment depends upon its size, location & rate of growth; options include regular monitoring with imaging tests, medication management especially antihypertensives, lifestyle changes such as quitting smoking/alcohol use, dietary modifications, surgery if necessary when at significant risk of rupturing.
answer_noisy: An abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) refers to a localized enlargement of the abdomardial segment of the aorta, which can lead to rupture if not monitored or treated appropriately due to weakened vessel walls. It's important for individuals with AAA risk factors such as age over 65, smoking history, male gender, family history, etc., to undergo regular screening via ultrasound imaging. Treatment options vary from watchful waiting with lifestyle modifications to surgical intervention depending upon size and growth rate of the aneurysm.
answer_repaired: An aneurysm in a major artery refers to a localized dilation or bulging of the vessel wall due to weakness; if ruptured, this can lead to life-threatening internal bleeding. It means that there's abnormal enlargement which may require monitoring or intervention depending on its size and location.

question_noisy: what does it usually mean when some1 suddenly has big problems talking or getting words out and maybe not understanding stuff right, is that like a general brain/language issue or just speech trouble?
question_repaired: What does sudden difficulty speaking, finding words, or understanding language usually indicate, and how can you tell whether it is a language problem in the brain or a speech problem?
reference_answer: Aphasia is a disorder caused by damage to the parts of the brain that control language. It can make it hard for you to read, write, and say what you mean to say. It is most common in adults who have had a stroke. Brain tumors, infections, injuries, and dementia can also cause it. The type of problem you have and how bad it is depends on which part of your brain is damaged and how much damage there is. There are four main types: - Expressive aphasia - you know what you want to say, but you have trouble saying or writing what you mean - Receptive aphasia - you hear the voice or see the print, but you can't make sense of the words - Anomic aphasia - you have trouble using the correct word for objects, places, or events - Global aphasia - you can't speak, understand speech, read, or write Some people recover from aphasia without treatment. Most, however, need language therapy as soon as possible. NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke
answer_clean: Aphasia is a communication disorder that results from damage to parts of the brain responsible for language production or processing. It can affect speaking, understanding speech, reading, writing, and other forms of expression. The severity varies depending on which areas of the brain have been affected by injury such as stroke, traumatic head wound, tumor, etc., leading individuals with this condition to experience difficulties in expressing themselves verbally or comprehending others' spoken words effectively. There exist different types of aphasias like Broca’s, Wernicke’s, Global, Anomic among others each characterized by distinct symptoms related to specific linguistic functions impaired due to localization of lesion within cerebral cortex involved in language abilities. Treatment typically involves speech-language therapy aimed at improving functional use of remaining capabilities while compensating lost skills through alternative strategies tailored according patient needs & goals alongside supportive care measures addressing emotional wellbeing impacted during recovery process.
answer_noisy: Sudden difficulty with speaking (expressive language) or comprehension could indicate an acute neurological event such as stroke; immediate evaluation by emergency services is crucial for proper diagnosis and treatment to minimize potential damage. This condition may involve various areas of the brain responsible for language processing but should be assessed individually through clinical examination and imaging studies if necessary. It's essential to seek urgent medical attention in these cases rather than self-diagnosing.
answer_repaired: Sudden difficulty with speaking, word-finding, or comprehension often indicates an acute neurological event such as stroke affecting areas of the brain responsible for language (aphasia). To differentiate between a primary language disorder (receptive/expressive) versus a motor speech issue like dysarthria or apraxia, one should assess both expressive abilities without gestures and understand spoken commands; if these fail but nonverbal communication remains intact, this suggests receptive/expressive aphasia rather than pure articulation issues which would be present even when using gestures to communicate needs.
